sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Maria Martin, a 74-year-old female, presents for a follow-up visit.
She reports her blood sugar levels are not well-controlled, and she has not increased her glimepiride as instructed.
She denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, chest pains, pressure or tightness, nausea, vomiting, and fever.
She reports feeling normal and has not noticed any significant changes in her condition.
She has had cataract surgery and is satisfied with the outcome, but has concerns about the new lens.
She mentions she has peripheral neuropathy and pain in her hips and upper legs.
She has been smoking and is not ready to quit.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of high blood pressure, high cholesterol, vascular disease in her legs, and diabetes.
She is on multiple medications including metoprolol 50 mg once a day, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20 with 25 mg once a day, amlodipine 10 mg once a day, and atorvastatin.
She has undergone cataract surgery and has had an eye exam recently.
Review of systems: The patient denies any headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, chest pains, pressure or tightness, nausea, vomiting, and fever.
She reports being able to urinate several times per day, but takes a little longer to start urinating and occasionally gets up at night to pee.
She drinks water in the evening.
Current medications:
Glimepiride 2 mg twice a day for diabetes
Metoprolol 50 mg once a day for high blood pressure
Lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20 with 25 mg once a day for high blood pressure
Amlodipine 10 mg once a day for high blood pressure
Atorvastatin for high cholesterol
Metformin for diabetes
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 140/60, pulse rate not mentioned, oxygen saturation not mentioned, temperature not mentioned.
Physical exam: Carotids are normal, pulses are normal, heart exam shows a regular rhythm and normal rate, S1 and S2 are normal, lungs are clear, no rashes or wheezing are present.

Extremities are without edema, pulses are palpable on the dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial, thickened nails are present, mildly dry skin is present, and mildly decreased sensation in the tips of the first and second toes.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has poorly controlled diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and vascular disease in her legs.
Problem list: Diabetes mellitus; hypertension; hyperlipidemia; peripheral vascular disease; peripheral neuropathy; smoking.
Plan:
I increased the patient's glimepiride to 4 mg twice a day and instructed her to take two of the 2 mg pills twice a day until she receives the new prescription.
I prescribed metformin to continue.
I advised her to watch for low blood sugar symptoms such as weakness, shakiness, and sweating.
I rechecked her blood pressure and it was 140/60.
I will continue her current medications and check her kidney function today.
I will also continue her high-dose atorvastatin to protect her arteries.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 4 months for a follow-up appointment.
